prompt:
You are an expert LLVM compiler engineer, specializing in LLVM IR, InstCombine, and missed-optimization analysis.

I found a **new missed optimization in InstCombine** and currently have **only one LLVM IR example**.

Your tasks:

1) Understand the missed optimization from my single example.
2) Generalize it into a reusable pattern and summarize the pattern so it could be implemented as an InstCombine fold.
3) Generate many additional LLVM IR test cases that are semantically equivalent and should trigger the same transformation, including corner cases.

## Reference: common missed elements and patterns

When generalizing, consider the following summary of elements frequently missed in InstCombine and patterns that are often not well handled. 

The original motivating case is a missed fold around a sign-bit extraction used as a boolean. It computes `%lobit = lshr i32 %x, 31`, truncates that to `i1`, then inverts/feeds it into a `select` with `icmp slt %x, 0`. Since `lshr %x, 31` is exactly the sign bit in bit 0, `trunc` should become the signed-negativity test directly; failing to do that leaves redundant IR and blocks the larger logical-select fold.

The patch generalizes this in `visitTrunc`: if the trunc source matches `shr X, (bitwidth-1)`—via the generic `m_Shr`, so both `lshr` and `ashr` are accepted—and the destination is `i1`, InstCombine replaces the trunc with `icmp slt X, 0`. Key abstraction points: match the canonical “shift by top bit” pattern, ignore whether the shift is logical or arithmetic, and produce a boolean compare that is easier for later combines to reason about.

The dev tests broaden coverage in two directions. In `trunc-lshr.ll`, they cover both `lshr` and `ashr`, both exact top-bit shifts (`31`) and non-top-bit shifts (`30`, should not fold), and memory/use-shape variations such as one vs multiple stores to ensure the transform is use-independent. In `logical-select.ll`, they verify downstream generalization of the motivating idiom into logical-select recognition: scalar and vector forms, bitcasted vector masks, commuted `and/or` structure, all-sign-bits masks, and poison-sensitive boolean vectors. This shows the optimization is not just for one scalar testcase, but a reusable canonicalization enabling broader select simplification.

## Output format requirement (strict)

Return **ONLY** JSON (a single JSON object). No Markdown, no extra text.

## What I will provide

- The original LLVM IR snippet (minimal reproducer).
- The target triple/datalayout if relevant. 

## What you must output (in JSON)

Include the following top-level keys (you may add more keys if useful):

- "pattern" (generalized pattern description)
- "testcases" (an array of testcase objects; include only positive cases)

Each testcase object must include at least:

- "name"
- "ir" (the input IR for this case)
- "expected_optimized_ir" (what InstCombine should produce, at least the key simplified instructions; full function preferred)

---

## Here is my single example 

define i1 @test(i32 %0, ptr writeonly captures(none) initializes((0, 4)) %p) local_unnamed_addr #0 {
common.ret1:
  %.lobit = lshr i32 %0, 31
  %1 = trunc nuw i32 %.lobit to i1
  %2 = icmp slt i32 %0, 0
  %not. = xor i1 %1, true
  %common.ret1.op = select i1 %not., i1 %2, i1 false
  store i32 %.lobit, ptr %p, align 1
  ret i1 %common.ret1.op
}

define i1 @test(i32 %0, ptr writeonly captures(none) initializes((0, 1)) %p) local_unnamed_addr #0 {
common.ret1:
  %.lobit = lshr i32 %0, 31
  store i32 %.lobit, ptr %p, align 1
  ret i1 false
}

pattern.summary: Fold sign-bit extraction through trunc-to-i1 into a signed-negative compare. If a value X of integer type iN is shifted right by exactly N-1 bits using either lshr or ashr, and that shifted result is truncated to i1, then the trunc is equivalent to `icmp slt X, 0`.

preconditions: X has integer scalar or integer vector type with element bitwidth N
destination type of trunc is i1 or vector of i1 with matching lane count
C is a constant integer or splat constant equal to N-1 for each lane
The transform is use-independent; source shift may have other uses

rewrite.scalar: trunc (shr X, N-1) to i1  ==>  icmp slt X, 0
rewrite.vector: trunc (shr <Ty> X, splat(N-1)) to <M x i1>  ==>  icmp slt <Ty> X, zeroinitializer

notes: Accept both `lshr` and `ashr`; both place the original sign bit into bit 0 when shifted by N-1.
Works for arbitrary integer widths, not just power-of-two widths.
Preserves semantics even if the trunc carries flags like `nuw`; those flags are irrelevant after folding.
This canonicalization often enables later folds of boolean logic/select patterns involving sign tests.
Only exact top-bit shifts should fold. Positive tests below all use the exact shift amount.

testcases.name: scalar_i32_lshr_basic
scalar_i32_lshr_with_nuw_trunc
scalar_i32_ashr_basic
scalar_i8_lshr
scalar_i8_ashr
scalar_i16_lshr
scalar_i64_lshr
scalar_i64_ashr
scalar_i17_lshr_odd_width
scalar_i33_ashr_odd_width
scalar_i128_lshr
scalar_with_extra_shift_use
testcases.ir:
define i1 @scalar_with_extra_shift_use(i32 %x, ptr %p) {
entry:
  %s = lshr i32 %x, 31
  store i32 %s, ptr %p, align 4
  %t = trunc i32 %s to i1
  ret i1 %t
}
testcases.expected_optimized_ir:
define i1 @scalar_with_extra_shift_use(i32 %x, ptr %p) {
entry:
  %s = lshr i32 %x, 31
  store i32 %s, ptr %p, align 4
  %t = icmp slt i32 %x, 0
  ret i1 %t
}

testcases.name: scalar_with_freeze_operand
testcases.ir:
define i1 @scalar_with_freeze_operand(i32 %x) {
entry:
  %fx = freeze i32 %x
  %s = lshr i32 %fx, 31
  %t = trunc i32 %s to i1
  ret i1 %t
}

testcases.name: scalar_inverted_boolean
testcases.ir:
define i1 @scalar_inverted_boolean(i32 %x) {
entry:
  %s = lshr i32 %x, 31
  %t = trunc i32 %s to i1
  %n = xor i1 %t, true
  ret i1 %n
}

testcases.name: motivating_select_fold_enabler
testcases.ir:
define i1 @motivating_select_fold_enabler(i32 %x, ptr %p) {
entry:
  %lobit = lshr i32 %x, 31
  %b = trunc i32 %lobit to i1
  %neg = icmp slt i32 %x, 0
  %notb = xor i1 %b, true
  %r = select i1 %notb, i1 %neg, i1 false
  store i32 %lobit, ptr %p, align 4
  ret i1 %r
}
testcases.expected_optimized_ir:
define i1 @motivating_select_fold_enabler(i32 %x, ptr %p) {
entry:
  %lobit = lshr i32 %x, 31
  %neg = icmp slt i32 %x, 0
  %notb = xor i1 %neg, true
  %r = select i1 %notb, i1 %neg, i1 false
  store i32 %lobit, ptr %p, align 4
  ret i1 %r
}

testcases.name: vector_v4i32_lshr
vector_v4i32_ashr
vector_v2i64_lshr
vector_v8i8_ashr
vector_v3i17_lshr_odd_width
vector_with_extra_shift_use
testcases.ir:
define <4 x i1> @vector_with_extra_shift_use(<4 x i32> %x, ptr %p) {
entry:
  %s = lshr <4 x i32> %x, <i32 31, i32 31, i32 31, i32 31>
  store <4 x i32> %s, ptr %p, align 16
  %t = trunc <4 x i32> %s to <4 x i1>
  ret <4 x i1> %t
}
testcases.expected_optimized_ir:
define <4 x i1> @vector_with_extra_shift_use(<4 x i32> %x, ptr %p) {
entry:
  %s = lshr <4 x i32> %x, <i32 31, i32 31, i32 31, i32 31>
  store <4 x i32> %s, ptr %p, align 16
  %t = icmp slt <4 x i32> %x, zeroinitializer
  ret <4 x i1> %t
}

testcases.name: vector_select_fold_enabler
testcases.ir:
define <4 x i1> @vector_select_fold_enabler(<4 x i32> %x) {
entry:
  %lobit = lshr <4 x i32> %x, <i32 31, i32 31, i32 31, i32 31>
  %b = trunc <4 x i32> %lobit to <4 x i1>
  %neg = icmp slt <4 x i32> %x, zeroinitializer
  %notb = xor <4 x i1> %b, <i1 true, i1 true, i1 true, i1 true>
  %r = select <4 x i1> %notb, <4 x i1> %neg, <4 x i1> zeroinitializer
  ret <4 x i1> %r
}
testcases.expected_optimized_ir:
define <4 x i1> @vector_select_fold_enabler(<4 x i32> %x) {
entry:
  %lobit = lshr <4 x i32> %x, <i32 31, i32 31, i32 31, i32 31>
  %neg = icmp slt <4 x i32> %x, zeroinitializer
  %notb = xor <4 x i1> %neg, <i1 true, i1 true, i1 true, i1 true>
  %r = select <4 x i1> %notb, <4 x i1> %neg, <4 x i1> zeroinitializer
  ret <4 x i1> %r
}

testcases.name: vector_fixed_nonpow2_lanes

  %s = lshr <4 x i32> %x, <i32 31, i32 31, i32 31, i32 31>
  store <4 x i32> %s, ptr %p, align 16
  %t = icmp slt <4 x i32> %x, zeroinitializer
  ret <4 x i1> %t
}

; ModuleID = '<stdin>'

define <4 x i1> @vector_with_extra_shift_use(<4 x i32> %x, ptr %p) {
entry:
  %s = lshr <4 x i32> %x, splat (i32 31)
  store <4 x i32> %s, ptr %p, align 16
  %t = trunc nuw <4 x i32> %s to <4 x i1>
  ret <4 x i1> %t
}
